L1.area: Dynamic array capacity management and memory reallocation logic

L2.description: A double free and use-after-free vulnerability exists in the capacity growth logic of a dynamically-sized array type. When the array has already spilled to heap storage and is asked to grow to a size equal to its current capacity, it incorrectly frees the existing backing memory without reallocating. This performs a double free immediately and leads to use-after-free on subsequent accesses to the array contents. An attacker who controls the target growth size may exploit this to obtain memory contents or achieve remote code execution.

L3.area: Heap capacity growth and deallocation logic in the stack-optimized dynamic array implementation
L3.description: A double free and use-after-free vulnerability exists in the capacity growth logic of a stack-optimized dynamic array. When the array has spilled its storage to the heap and is asked to grow to a capacity exactly equal to its current heap capacity, the conditional branching in the growth method fails to handle this edge case and falls through to a deallocation path that frees the still-referenced backing storage. An attacker who controls the requested growth capacity can exploit this to obtain memory contents or achieve remote code execution.

ground_truth.reason: The capacity growth method deallocates the existing heap-allocated backing storage when called with a size equal to the current capacity, causing a double free and use-after-free on subsequent accesses.
